topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy printout of the lease agreement lay crumpled on the coffee table, a stark, unwelcome map of imminent separation. I stared at the address—a city three time zones away—and felt a slow, heavy pressure build behind my eyes. Liam, my anchor, was actually packing up his life, trading our shared apartment and late-night pizza runs for a life somewhere entirely unknown. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over his contact, before letting the device drop onto the cushion beside me with a dull thud. I didn't call him. I just sat there, the silence in the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Every perfectly organized shelf in the living room suddenly seemed like a monument to something I was actively dismantling.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually moving?" The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I leaned back against the doorframe, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a gesture that felt ridiculously defensive. I watched Sarah pace the worn rug, her movements jerky and agitated. She kept glancing at the moving boxes stacked in the hallway, her jaw working silently. I tried to keep my voice level, to sound casual, but the tightness in my throat betrayed me. It wasn't just that she was leaving; it was the sudden, jarring realization that the rhythm of our existence, the established choreography of our friendship, was about to be ripped apart by geography. I just needed a minute, a space where the sheer scale of the impending change wouldn't feel like a physical weight crushing my ribs.

[story 3]

I reread the text message for the tenth time. The casual tone he’d used to announce it felt like a deliberate cruelty. My hands started shaking, a fine tremor running up my forearms. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing to do something physical to counteract the swirling confusion in my head. I walked over to the window, looking out at the familiar street, but everything seemed muted, distant, as if viewed through thick, distorting glass. I clenched my fists until my knuckles turned white, fighting the urge to shout, to demand an explanation that felt impossible to articulate. Just the sheer fact of the distance, the miles separating us, was enough to make my breath catch in my throat.
